tier_1_regimens.notes: Tebentafusp-tebn is a bispecific gp100-HLA-A*02:01-directed T-cell receptor and CD3 T-cell engager. Treatment resulted in longer overall survival compared to investigator's choice of pembrolizumab, ipilimumab, or dacarbazine. Response rates are low, so symptomatic patients may be better palliated by liver-directed treatment first or may respond better to ipilimumab/nivolumab. [526, 537]

trial_data.trial_name: Phase II trials (Pelster et al, GEM-1402)

tier_1_regimens.notes: Nivolumab/ipilimumab has shown modest improvement in OS over historical benchmarks of chemotherapy. The dose used is nivolumab 1 mg/kg and ipilimumab 3 mg/kg. It is unknown whether other dosing regimens are equally effective. [534, 535]

drugs.name: Ipilimumab
drugs.schedule: every 3 weeks for 4 cycles, then every 12 weeks

trial_data.trial_name: Phase II studies (Piulats et al, Zimmer et al)

tier_1_regimens.notes: Ipilimumab monotherapy has shown limited clinical activity in metastatic uveal melanoma. Response rates are low. [527, 528]
Pembrolizumab is an anti-PD-1 antibody. It has shown low response rates in uveal melanoma. [530, 531]
Nivolumab monotherapy is an option for patients with disease progression on ipilimumab. [532, 533]
Dacarbazine is a cytotoxic chemotherapy with low response rates in uveal melanoma. [522, 524]
Temozolomide is an oral alkylating agent with low response rates. [547]
Paclitaxel is a taxane chemotherapy with low response rates. [539]
Albumin-bound paclitaxel is a nanoparticle formulation of paclitaxel. [540]
Carboplatin/paclitaxel is a combination chemotherapy regimen. [548]
Trametinib is a MEK inhibitor. It is included based on positive data for selumetinib and the general lack of systemic therapy options. [555, 563]

trial_data.trial_name: Phase II and phase III trials

tier_1_regimens.notes: Melphalan percutaneous hepatic perfusion is a liver-directed therapy for patients with hepatic-dominant metastatic uveal melanoma with <50% liver involvement. It requires overnight stay in ICU for hemodynamic monitoring and should be performed only at experienced centers. [472, 473]

tier_2_handbook.definition: Uveal melanoma is the most common primary intraocular malignancy in adults, arising from melanocytes within the uveal tract, which includes the choroid (~85% of cases), ciliary body, and iris. It is a distinct biological entity from cutaneous and conjunctival melanomas, with different patterns of presentation, prognosis, molecular signatures, and management [1, 20]. The tumors originate from melanocytes of neural crest origin that populate the uvea. Anatomically, the uveal tract forms the middle vascular layer of the eye. The choroid is the posterior part, the ciliary body is the middle part (containing the ciliary muscle), and the iris is the anterior part. This guideline specifically addresses melanomas of the choroid and ciliary body; iris melanomas are excluded due to their distinct characteristics and lower metastatic risk [1].

subtypes.name: Choroidal Melanoma
subtypes.description: Arises in the choroid, the most common site (~85% of uveal melanomas). Typically presents as a pigmented, dome-shaped or mushroom-shaped lesion after rupture of Bruch's membrane.
key_associations: Higher metastatic potential than iris melanoma

subtypes.name: Ciliary Body Melanoma
subtypes.description: Arises in the ciliary body. Can be hidden behind the iris, leading to delayed diagnosis. May cause lens displacement, cataract, or elevated intraocular pressure. Often associated with dilated episcleral 'sentinel' vessels.
subtypes.frequency: Less common than choroidal, more common than iris.
key_associations: Included in the primary Uveal Melanoma NCCN guideline

subtypes.name: Iris Melanoma
subtypes.description: Arises in the iris. Generally has a better prognosis and a lower rate of metastasis (~5% at 5 years). Has a different AJCC staging system and molecular signature.
key_associations: Different treatment considerations due to anatomy

subtypes.name: Spindle Cell Melanoma (G1)
subtypes.description: Histologic subtype composed of >90% spindle cells. Generally considered less aggressive.
subtypes.frequency: Not specified as a frequency, but part of the G1 grade.
key_associations: Better prognosis compared to epithelioid dominant tumors

subtypes.name: Mixed Cell Melanoma (G2)
subtypes.description: Histologic subtype with >10% epithelioid cells and <90% spindle cells.
subtypes.frequency: Not specified as a frequency, but part of the G2 grade.

subtypes.name: Epithelioid Cell Melanoma (G3)
subtypes.description: Histologic subtype composed of >90% epithelioid cells. Generally considered more aggressive.
subtypes.frequency: Not specified as a frequency, but part of the G3 grade.

subtypes.name: Class 1 Uveal Melanoma (Gene Expression Profile - GEP)
subtypes.description: A molecular subtype defined by a 15-gene expression profile (15-GEP). Class 1A or Class 1B with/without PRAME expression carries a lower to intermediate risk of metastasis.
key_associations: PRAME expression can modify risk within this class [34, 394-396]

subtypes.name: Class 2 Uveal Melanoma (Gene Expression Profile - GEP)
subtypes.description: A molecular subtype defined by a 15-gene expression profile (15-GEP). Associated with a 5-fold to 20-fold higher risk of metastasis compared to Class 1.
key_associations: Often associated with monosomy 3 and BAP1 mutation [34, 397-399]

tier_2_handbook.molecular_pathogenesis: Uveal melanoma is characterized by distinct genomic events that drive tumorigenesis and inform prognosis. Over 80% of tumors harbor mutually exclusive activating mutations in the G-protein alpha subunits GNAQ or GNA11, which lead to constitutive activation of the RAS/RAF/MEK/ERK (MAPK) pathway [23, 47, 48]. Further genetic progression is defined by mutations in specific genes that correlate with metastatic risk. Loss of chromosome 3 (monosomy 3) and gain of chromosome 8q, especially with numerous copies, are strong indicators of high metastatic risk [13, 45, 401, 407-413]. Somatic mutations in the BAP1 tumor suppressor gene (observed in ~50% of uveal melanomas) are strongly associated with high-risk, metastasizing tumors, particularly in the context of Class 2 GEP and monosomy 3 [49, 50, 398, 417, 418]. In contrast, mutations in EIF1AX (found in up to 20% of tumors) are associated with lower risk of metastasis [25, 51, 52, 398, 420-424]. Mutations in SF3B1 (present in ~20% of tumors) are associated with a medium risk of metastasis, with some studies reporting they may lead to late metastases [25, 51, 420, 421, 424]. Other chromosomal abnormalities linked to risk include gain of chromosome 6p (low risk) and loss of 8p [13, 34, 398, 400, 401, 414-416]. PRAME expression is a risk modifier to the 15-GEP signal and may be associated with an increased risk of metastasis in both Class 1 and Class 2 tumors [34, 394-396]. Importantly, BRAF, NRAS, and KIT mutations are extremely rare in uveal melanoma, distinguishing it molecularly from cutaneous melanoma [23, 35, 36].

epidemiology.incidence: Uveal melanoma is the most common primary intraocular malignancy in adults, with an incidence of approximately 5 people per million annually in the United States [1, 2]. The distribution by subsite is ~85% choroidal, with the remainder in the iris or ciliary body [2]. The incidence differs from cutaneous melanoma and varies by race/ethnicity; it is more common in white populations compared to other racial groups.
epidemiology.mortality: Most uveal melanomas are localized at first presentation, with <3% presenting with metastases [3]. The 5-year disease-specific survival (DSS) is 70% to 81% overall. For early-stage disease (stage I–II), 5-year DSS is ≥85% [4, 6, 7]. For those with distant metastatic disease, most studies report an estimated 5-year survival of <20%, which has not improved over recent decades [4, 6, 8-12].
epidemiology.trends: The document does not provide specific temporal incidence or mortality trends data.
epidemiology.demographics: The distribution of uveal melanoma by sex, race, and geography differs from cutaneous melanoma [1]. It is more common in white populations. Specific age-adjusted incidence or sex ratio data are not provided in the excerpted source text.

risk_factors.detail: Choroidal nevi occur in 1.9% to 6.5% of the population and can transform into melanoma. One study reported 8% of diagnosed uveal melanomas arose from a previously documented nevus [55-59].
This condition, causing hyperpigmentation of the episclera, uvea, and skin, has a much higher rate in patients with uveal melanoma than in the general population [83-88].

Familial cases occur and are associated with germline pathogenic variants in BAP1 (BAP1 Tumor Predisposition Syndrome), PALB2, POT1, or MBD4. BAP1 syndrome increases risk for uveal melanoma, mesothelioma, cutaneous melanoma, and renal cell carcinoma. Genetic counseling is recommended for patients with early-onset disease or personal/family history of associated cancers [1-9, 89-105, 106-111].

risk_factors.factor: Light Skin Color, Propensity to Sunburn, Light Iris Color
risk_factors.detail: These phenotypic traits are associated with increased uveal melanoma risk [10, 112-115].

risk_factors.factor: Strong Personal or Family History of Cancer
risk_factors.detail: A strong personal or family history of cancer should trigger evaluation for hereditary syndromes like BAP1, BRCA1/BRCA2, or Lynch syndrome [b, 11, 121].
An occupational history of welding has been associated with increased uveal melanoma risk, with a dose-response relationship with job duration [12, 116].

risk_factors.factor: Specific Genetic Syndromes (BIRT-HOGG-DUBÉ, Myotonic Dystrophy, Immunocompromise)
risk_factors.detail: Patient characteristics that increase the likelihood of choroidal nevi growth or choroidal/ocular melanoma include Birt-Hogg-Dubé syndrome, myotonic dystrophy, and immunocompromise [76-82].

clinical_features.typical_presentation: Uveal melanoma typically presents as a pigmented lesion in the choroid (approximately 85% of cases) or ciliary body. The most common symptom is blurred vision, but patients may also experience visual field defects, photopsia, floaters, metamorphopsia, eye irritation, pain, redness, or a sensation of pressure. Approximately 13% to 30% of patients are asymptomatic at diagnosis, discovered incidentally during routine eye examinations. Physical signs include a dome-shaped (75%) or mushroom-shaped (20%) pigmented mass, often with subretinal fluid (present in 75% of cases) and orange pigment (lipofuscin). Ciliary body melanomas may be hidden behind the iris, leading to delayed presentation until the tumor is large, causing lens tilting, cataract, elevated intraocular pressure, or dilated episcleral 'sentinel' vessels. Risk factors that increase suspicion include choroidal nevi, ocular/oculodermal melanocytosis, familial uveal melanoma (germline pathogenic variants in BAP1, PALB2, POT1, or MBD4), light skin and eye color, propensity to sunburn, occupational welding history, or strong personal/family history of cancer (e.g., BRCA1/2-associated cancers). The presence of cutaneous melanoma does not increase the risk of uveal melanoma.

symptoms.name: Blurred vision
symptoms.detail: Most common symptom; may be due to tumor location affecting the macula or retina.
symptoms.frequency: Majority of symptomatic patients; reported in studies as the primary complaint.
symptoms.alarm: New or progressive blurred vision warrants urgent ophthalmologic evaluation.

symptoms.name: Visual field defect/loss
symptoms.detail: Can occur if the tumor involves the peripheral retina or optic nerve.
symptoms.frequency: Common; reported in a significant proportion of symptomatic cases.
symptoms.alarm: Sudden or progressive field loss requires immediate investigation.

symptoms.name: Photopsia
symptoms.detail: Flashes of light, often due to retinal traction or irritation.
symptoms.frequency: Frequently reported; associated with retinal involvement.
symptoms.alarm: Persistent or increasing photopsia is a red flag for retinal pathology.

symptoms.name: Floaters
symptoms.detail: May result from hemorrhage or inflammatory cells in the vitreous.
symptoms.frequency: Common; can be an early sign of tumor growth or complications.
symptoms.alarm: New floaters, especially with visual changes, necessitate evaluation.

symptoms.name: Metamorphopsia
symptoms.detail: Distorted vision, often due to macular involvement or subretinal fluid.
symptoms.frequency: Less common but significant; indicates central retinal impact.
symptoms.alarm: Any distortion of vision is an urgent concern.

symptoms.name: Eye irritation, pain, or redness
symptoms.detail: May be associated with ciliary body involvement or secondary glaucoma.
symptoms.frequency: More common in ciliary body melanomas; can mimic inflammatory conditions.
symptoms.alarm: Unexplained ocular pain or redness requires prompt assessment to rule out malignancy.

symptoms.name: Asymptomatic presentation
symptoms.detail: Discovered incidentally during routine eye exams; tumors may be small and peripheral.
symptoms.alarm: Even asymptomatic pigmented lesions need careful monitoring for growth or high-risk features.

signs.name: Pigmented uveal mass
signs.detail: Size and shape are critical for staging and treatment planning.
signs.frequency: Universal in uveal melanoma; size varies with tumor stage.

signs.name: Subretinal fluid
signs.detail: Supports diagnosis; rare in benign choroidal nevi.
signs.frequency: Present in 75% of patients with uveal melanoma.

signs.name: Orange pigment (lipofuscin)
signs.detail: Highly autofluorescent on imaging; associated with malignancy.
signs.frequency: Common; predictive of growth in indeterminate lesions.

signs.name: Ciliary body involvement
signs.detail: Worsens prognosis; documented for staging and treatment selection.
signs.frequency: Variable; increases with tumor size and T stage.

signs.name: Extraocular extension
signs.detail: Indicates advanced disease; requires MRI for assessment.
signs.frequency: Present in approximately 3% at diagnosis; up to 12% in T4 tumors.

signs.name: Sentinel vessels
signs.detail: Visible on slit-lamp examination; may indicate tumor activity.

atypical_or_missed: Ciliary body melanomas hidden behind the iris, leading to delayed diagnosis until symptoms like glaucoma or lens displacement occur.
Amelanotic (nonpigmented) tumors that may be mistaken for other conditions like hemorrhage or metastasis.
Diffuse, flat, plateau-shaped, or multinodular 'tapioca' appearance variants that can mimic inflammatory or vascular lesions.
Small lesions with few risk factors, observed as indeterminate, which may slowly transform into melanoma.
Metastasis to the uvea from other cancers (e.g., breast, lung) that can mimic primary uveal melanoma.

red_flags: New or progressive visual symptoms (blurred vision, field loss, photopsia, floaters) in an adult.
Presence of a pigmented uveal mass with subretinal fluid or orange pigment on imaging.
Tumor growth documented on serial examinations (e.g., increase in thickness >2 mm or diameter >5 mm).
Ciliary body involvement, especially with secondary glaucoma or sentinel vessels.
Extraocular extension suspected on clinical exam or imaging.
High-risk features on biopsy (e.g., monosomy 3, BAP1 mutation, Class 2 gene expression profile).
Symptoms of metastatic disease (e.g., abdominal pain, weight loss) in a patient with known uveal melanoma.

diagnostic.test: Comprehensive eye exam (biomicroscopy and dilated fundus exam)
diagnostic.rationale: Initial assessment to detect and characterize the lesion; includes visual acuity measurement, tumor size/location documentation, and evaluation for subretinal fluid or orange pigment.
diagnostic.key_findings: Pigmented mass, subretinal fluid, orange pigment, tumor margins, ciliary body involvement.

diagnostic.rationale: Essential for diagnosing choroidal melanomas; provides tumor dimensions, internal reflectivity (low in melanoma), and detects extraocular extension.
diagnostic.key_findings: Low internal reflectivity on A-scan, acoustic hollowing on B-scan, choroidal excavation, orbital shadowing.

diagnostic.rationale: Documents clinical features, measures basal diameter and area, and identifies orange pigment or drusen.
diagnostic.key_findings: Visual record of tumor shape, color, and associated features; correlates with autofluorescence.

diagnostic.rationale: Helps distinguish orange pigment (hyperautofluorescent) from drusen; aids in differential diagnosis.
diagnostic.key_findings: Hyperautofluorescence in orange pigment; patterns can differentiate melanoma from nevi.

diagnostic.rationale: Evaluates retinal and RPE changes; detects subretinal fluid, edema, and structural loss in melanoma.
diagnostic.key_findings: Subretinal fluid, shaggy photoreceptors, RPE atrophy; enhanced depth imaging (EDI-OCT) useful for small tumors.

diagnostic.rationale: Characterizes tumor vasculature; may show 'double circulation' (intrinsic tumor circulation plus choroidal vasculature).
diagnostic.key_findings: Intrinsic tumor circulation, vascular abnormalities; useful for diagnosing and monitoring treatment complications.

diagnostic.rationale: Used when other methods are inadequate; helps measure tumor dimensions in large ciliary body melanomas.
diagnostic.key_findings: Shadowing artifacts; may overestimate tumor size compared to histology.

diagnostic.test: MRI of the orbit (with and without IV contrast)
diagnostic.rationale: Indicated for large tumors, proximity to optic nerve, or suspected extraocular extension; confirms extraocular involvement and aids in treatment planning.
diagnostic.key_findings: High signal on T1-weighted images, low on T2-weighted; detects extraocular extension.

diagnostic.test: Biopsy of primary tumor (fine-needle aspiration or vitrectomy-assisted)
diagnostic.rationale: For prognostic analysis when diagnosis is uncertain or to inform risk stratification and clinical trial eligibility; typically performed before irradiation.
diagnostic.key_findings: Cytologic/histologic confirmation; molecular/chromosomal testing for prognosis preferred over cytology alone.

staging.test: Contrast-enhanced MRI of the liver (preferred) or ultrasound
staging.rationale: Liver is the most common site of metastasis; imaging screens for distant disease at baseline and follow-up.
staging.indication: All patients prior to treatment; surveillance based on risk stratification.

staging.rationale: Additional imaging to evaluate for metastasis in lungs, bones, or other sites; limit radiation exposure.
staging.indication: Considered for baseline staging or if MRI contraindicated.

staging.test: Chest X-ray or CT chest without contrast
staging.rationale: Screens for lung metastases; less sensitive than CT with contrast but reduces radiation.
staging.indication: Part of surveillance imaging for metastasis.

staging.rationale: Can detect metastatic disease but has limited sensitivity for liver lesions; used in select cases.
staging.indication: Considered if other imaging inconclusive or for comprehensive staging.

staging.rationale: Only if neurological symptoms present; routine CNS imaging not recommended.
staging.indication: Symptomatic patients to rule out brain metastases.

biomarkers.rationale: Classifies tumors into Class 1A (low risk), Class 1B (medium risk), or Class 2 (high risk) for metastasis; informs surveillance frequency.
biomarkers.when: Strongly considered during primary tumor biopsy for prognostic analysis.
biomarkers.mandatory: No, but recommended for risk stratification and clinical trial eligibility.

biomarkers.test: Chromosomal testing (e.g., FISH for monosomy 3, gain of 6p, 8q)
biomarkers.rationale: Monosomy 3 and gain of 8q are high-risk; disomy 3 and gain of 6p are low-risk.
biomarkers.when: Performed on biopsy tissue; part of molecular prognostication.
biomarkers.mandatory: No, but used in conjunction with GEP for comprehensive risk assessment.

biomarkers.rationale: BAP1 somatic mutation indicates high risk; SF3B1 mutation medium risk; EIF1AX mutation low risk.
biomarkers.when: During biopsy for prognostication; may guide follow-up and treatment.
biomarkers.mandatory: No, but part of molecular profiling.

biomarkers.rationale: PRAME is a risk modifier; its expression may increase metastasis risk in both Class 1 and Class 2 tumors.
biomarkers.mandatory: No, but emerging as an important prognostic marker.

biomarkers.rationale: Determines eligibility for tebentafusp-tebn therapy in metastatic uveal melanoma.
biomarkers.when: For patients with distant metastatic disease.

biomarkers.test: Liver function tests (LFTs) including LDH and alkaline phosphatase
biomarkers.rationale: LDH and ALP may assist in prognosis in metastatic disease; poor sensitivity for early detection of liver metastases.
biomarkers.when: Considered during follow-up and for metastatic workup.

do_not_miss: Ocular ultrasound for tumor characterization in any suspicious pigmented lesion.
Contrast-enhanced MRI of the liver for staging and surveillance, as it is preferred over CT for liver metastasis detection.
Prognostic molecular testing (GEP, chromosomal analysis) if biopsy is performed, to inform risk stratification.
HLA-A*02:01 testing in metastatic disease for eligibility for tebentafusp.
Documentation of ciliary body involvement and extraocular extension, as they impact treatment and prognosis.
avoid: Routine brain MRI without neurological symptoms, as CNS metastases are rare (4-5%) and routine imaging is not recommended.
Frequent CT scans without contrast for surveillance due to cumulative radiation exposure; prefer MRI or ultrasound for liver imaging.
Overuse of liver function tests for early detection of metastases due to low sensitivity.
Biopsy for prognostic analysis if not discussed or if risks outweigh benefits (e.g., in very small tumors with low suspicion).
Imaging beyond standard guidelines without clinical indication, to limit unnecessary radiation and healthcare costs.

staging.system: AJCC 8th edition TNM staging system for choroidal and ciliary body melanoma.

t_stages.description: Tumor size category 1 (based on thickness and largest basal diameter; see Figure 1). Substages: T1a (without ciliary body involvement or extraocular extension), T1b (with ciliary body involvement), T1c (without ciliary body involvement but with extraocular extension ≤5 mm in largest diameter), T1d (with ciliary body involvement and extraocular extension ≤5 mm in largest diameter).
Tumor size category 2. Substages: T2a, T2b, T2c, T2d as per T1 pattern.
Tumor size category 3. Substages: T3a, T3b, T3c, T3d as per T1 pattern.
Tumor size category 4. Substages: T4a, T4b, T4c, T4d as per T1 pattern; T4e (any tumor size category with extraocular extension >5 mm in largest diameter).

n_stages.description: Regional lymph nodes cannot be assessed.

Regional lymph node metastases or discrete tumor deposits in the orbit. Substages: N1a (metastasis in one or more regional lymph nodes), N1b (no regional lymph nodes positive, but discrete tumor deposits in the orbit not contiguous to the eye).

m_stages.description: Distant metastasis. Substages: M1a (largest diameter of largest metastasis ≤3.0 cm), M1b (3.1–8.0 cm), M1c (≥8.1 cm).

stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Small tumor without ciliary body involvement or extraocular extension; low risk of metastasis.
stage_groups.five_yr_survival: ≥85% disease-specific survival (DSS) for stages I-II combined.
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Curative; options include observation, brachytherapy, particle beam radiation, or enucleation based on tumor size.

stage_groups.criteria: T1b-d, N0, M0 or T2a, N0, M0
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Small tumor with ciliary body involvement or limited extraocular extension, or medium tumor without high-risk features.
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Curative; primary treatment with radiation or enucleation.

stage_groups.criteria: T2b, N0, M0 or T3a, N0, M0
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Medium tumor with ciliary body involvement or large tumor without high-risk features.
stage_groups.five_yr_survival: Not explicitly stated; intermediate risk between stage I and III.

stage_groups.criteria: T2c-d, N0, M0 or T3b-c, N0, M0 or T4a, N0, M0
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Medium or large tumor with limited extraocular extension or ciliary body involvement.
stage_groups.five_yr_survival: 5-year risk of metastasis up to 44% for stage III.
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Curative with aggressive local therapy; may require adjuvant radiation.

stage_groups.criteria: T3d, N0, M0 or T4b-c, N0, M0
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Large tumor with high-risk features like ciliary body involvement or significant extraocular extension.
stage_groups.five_yr_survival: High risk; 5-year DSS may be lower than stage I-II.
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Curative; enucleation often recommended, with possible adjuvant radiation.

stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Very large tumor with extensive extraocular extension (>5 mm).
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Aggressive local treatment; enucleation with orbital exenteration if needed, and systemic therapy consideration.

stage_groups.criteria: Any T, N1, M0 or Any T, Any N, M1a-c
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Regional lymph node metastases or distant metastatic disease.
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Palliative; systemic therapy, liver-directed therapy, clinical trials.

staging_pearls: T stage is based on tumor thickness and largest basal diameter, with substages for ciliary body involvement and extraocular extension.
The cutoff for largest basal diameter for brachytherapy is ≤19 mm due to plaque size limitations; tumors >19 mm may require particle beam or enucleation.
Extraocular extension >5 mm (T4e) is a high-risk feature necessitating aggressive treatment.
Risk stratification for surveillance uses the highest risk factor present (e.g., T stage, GEP class, chromosomal abnormalities).
AJCC staging incorporates both anatomic extent and molecular markers for prognosis, but not all patients have biopsy for molecular testing.
Contralateral eye is not at increased risk; follow-up focuses on the affected eye and systemic surveillance.

management_principles.overview: The management of uveal melanoma requires a multidisciplinary approach tailored to the unique characteristics of the disease. The NCCN guidelines emphasize that uveal melanoma is distinct from cutaneous melanoma in its pathogenesis, molecular profile, clinical behavior, and treatment. The primary goal is to achieve local tumor control while preserving the eye and visual function whenever possible. However, the risk of distant metastasis is the predominant concern and drives the intensity of follow-up and systemic therapy considerations. Referral to centers with expertise in the management of uveal melanoma is recommended upon clinical presentation.

by_intent.patient_group: Patients with a suspicious pigmented uveal lesion.
by_intent.approach: Comprehensive clinical evaluation, including dilated fundus exam and ocular imaging (ultrasound, MRI if indicated), is essential for diagnosis. A biopsy should be strongly considered for prognostic analysis (e.g., gene expression profiling, chromosomal testing) to inform risk stratification and follow-up frequency.

by_intent.patient_group: Patients with confirmed primary uveal melanoma.
by_intent.approach: Treatment is selected based on tumor size, location, extraocular extension, and patient factors. Options include various forms of radiation therapy (plaque brachytherapy, particle beam, stereotactic radiosurgery) and enucleation. The trend is toward globe-conserving therapies.

by_intent.patient_group: All patients after treatment for primary uveal melanoma.
by_intent.approach: Follow-up is stratified by the risk of distant metastasis (low, medium, high) based on AJCC T stage and molecular markers. Surveillance includes regular ophthalmologic exams of the treated eye and systemic imaging focused on the liver, the most common metastatic site.

by_intent.patient_group: Patients with recurrent or distant metastatic uveal melanoma.
by_intent.approach: Management is challenging and often involves clinical trials. Options include liver-directed therapies for hepatic-dominant disease, systemic therapies (tebentafusp-tebn for HLA-A*02:01-positive patients, immune checkpoint inhibitors, chemotherapy), and best supportive care.

management_principles.mdt_requirements: Care should involve a multidisciplinary team including an ophthalmic oncologist, radiation oncologist, medical oncologist, and pathologist with expertise in uveal melanoma. For radiation therapy, an experienced brachytherapy or particle beam physicist is also required.
management_principles.performance_status_guide: Patient performance status and overall health are critical factors in treatment selection, especially when considering aggressive local therapies or systemic treatments for metastatic disease.

management_pathways.title: Evaluation of Suspicious Pigmented Uveal Lesion

nodes.criteria: Suspicious pigmented uveal tumor of ciliary body and/or choroid with uncertain diagnosis, especially for small tumors, and/or <3 risk factors for growth.

options.label: Observe and re-evaluate for growth or features of malignancy

management_pathways.title: Primary Treatment for Small Uveal Melanoma (Largest diameter ≤19 mm, thickness <2.5 mm)

options.label: Regular ophthalmologic follow-up in select patients
options.preference: For select patients with very low-risk features

options.label: Brachytherapy plaque
Particle beam radiation
Other options in highly select patients (e.g., laser treatment or enucleation per patient preference)
options.preference: Alternative for patients not candidates for standard RT

management_pathways.title: Primary Treatment for Medium Uveal Melanoma (Largest diameter ≤19 mm, thickness 2.5–10 mm)

options.label: Brachytherapy plaque
Particle beam radiation
Enucleation
options.preference: Recommended for neovascular glaucoma, tumor replacing >50% of globe, or blind, painful eyes

adjuvant_logic.summary: Additional treatment with resection, laser ablation, transpupillary thermotherapy, or cryotherapy can be considered if concern that adequate response was not achieved from initial radiation.

management_pathways.title: Primary Treatment for Large Uveal Melanoma (Largest diameter >19 mm or thickness >10 mm)

nodes.criteria: Largest diameter >19 mm (any thickness) or Thickness >10 mm (any diameter) or Thickness >8 mm with optic nerve involvement.

options.label: Particle beam radiation or Stereotactic radiosurgery (SRS)
options.preference: Plaque brachytherapy is NOT suitable for tumors exceeding plaque size limits.

options.label: Enucleation
options.preference: Considered, especially for extensive extraocular extension or poor visual prognosis

management_pathways.title: Management of Extraocular Extension Found at Enucleation

nodes.criteria: Microscopically positive or close margins after enucleation (but no clinical, intraoperative, or radiographic evidence of gross residual disease).

options.label: Observe
Consider RT to orbit
Biopsy extraocular tissue if possible and consider: Observation, Local excision, RT to orbit, or Orbital exenteration

management_pathways.title: Follow-up and Surveillance for Distant Metastasis
branching_basis: risk of distant metastasis (based on T stage, molecular markers)

nodes.criteria: Low risk: Class 1A or Class 1 PRAME (-), Disomy 3, Gain of chromosome 6p, EIF1AX mutation, T1 (AJCC).

options.label: Consider surveillance imaging every 12 months for 5 years, or as clinically indicated.
options.preference: For patients who elect to have surveillance

nodes.criteria: Medium risk: Class 1B or Class 1 PRAME (+), SF3B1 mutation, T2 and T3 (AJCC).

options.label: Consider surveillance imaging every 6–12 months for 10 years, then as clinically indicated.
options.preference: For patients who elect to have surveillance

nodes.criteria: High risk: Class 2, Monosomy 3, Gain of chromosome 8q, BAP1 somatic mutation, T4 (AJCC).

options.label: Consider surveillance imaging every 3–6 months for 5 years, then every 6–12 months for years 6–10, then as clinically indicated.
options.preference: For patients who elect to have surveillance

nodes.criteria: Intraocular recurrence (limited to eye, no orbital involvement).

options.label: RT (plaque brachytherapy or particle beam)
Enucleation
Laser ablation (e.g., transpupillary thermotherapy for small recurrences in patients who cannot undergo RT or surgery)

management_pathways.title: Treatment of Distant Metastatic Disease: Hepatic-Dominant

nodes.criteria: Hepatic-dominant disease (bulk of metastatic disease confined to liver).

options.label: Consider regional isolation perfusion (melphalan percutaneous hepatic perfusion), embolization (chemotherapy, radiation, immunotherapy), ablative procedures, resection and/or RT.
options.preference: Primary local option for hepatic-dominant disease

options.label: Tebentafusp-tebn (if HLA-A*02:01-positive) or Immune checkpoint inhibitors (e.g., ipilimumab + nivolumab)
Best supportive/palliative care
options.preference: For patients not candidates for active therapy

management_pathways.title: Treatment of Distant Metastatic Disease: Non-Hepatic-Dominant

nodes.criteria: Non-hepatic-dominant disease or disease not amenable to liver-directed therapy.

options.label: Tebentafusp-tebn (if HLA-A*02:01-positive) or Immune checkpoint inhibitors (e.g., ipilimumab + nivolumab)
Best supportive/palliative care
options.preference: For patients not candidates for active therapy

items.text: History and physical (H&P), including personal/family history of prior or current cancers (outside the eye).
items.recommendation: Required for all patients with a suspicious uveal lesion.

items.text: Comprehensive eye exam: Examine front and back of eye (biomicroscopy), perform dilated fundus exam (indirect ophthalmoscopy), measure visual acuity.
items.recommendation: Required for diagnosis and treatment planning.

items.text: Measure and document location and size of tumor (diameter, thickness), distance from disc and fovea, ciliary body involvement, subretinal fluid, and orange pigment.
items.recommendation: Essential for staging and treatment selection.

items.text: Ocular ultrasound (A-scan and B-scan, including ultrasound biomicroscopy for anterior lesions).
MRI with and without IV contrast as clinically indicated to confirm diagnosis (e.g., for large tumors, suspicion of extraocular extension).
Consider biopsy of primary tumor for prognostic analysis (molecular/chromosomal testing preferred over cytology alone).
items.recommendation: Strongly consider before radiation. Risks/benefits should be discussed.

items.text: If biopsy is performed, molecular/chromosomal testing (e.g., GEP, FISH for chromosomes 3, 6, 8; BAP1, SF3B1, EIF1AX mutations; PRAME expression) is preferred over cytology alone.
items.recommendation: For risk stratification and follow-up frequency.

items.text: Baseline imaging to screen for distant disease. At minimum, all patients should have contrast-enhanced MRI (preferred) or ultrasound of the liver.
items.recommendation: Required. Modality preference determined by expertise at treating institution.

items.text: Additional imaging: chest/abdomen/pelvis CT with and without contrast.
items.recommendation: Additional modality. Should limit radiation exposure.
For patients with metastatic disease to determine eligibility for tebentafusp-tebn.

items.text: Consider liver function tests (LFTs), including lactate dehydrogenase (LDH).
items.recommendation: May assist in prognosis, especially in metastatic setting.

items.text: Referral to centers with expertise in the management of uveal melanoma.

surgery.role: Surgery (enucleation) is a primary treatment option for uveal melanoma, particularly for large tumors, tumors with extensive extraocular extension, or when globe-conserving radiation therapy is not feasible or has failed. It is also used for ocular recurrence.
principles: While there is a trend toward avoiding enucleation, it is recommended for patients with neovascular glaucoma, tumor replacing >50% of globe, or blind, painful eyes.
Consider enucleation in cases of extensive extraocular extension.
Pathologic evaluation should follow the uveal melanoma synoptic report recommendations by the College of American Pathologists.
Orbital exenteration is considered for visible extraocular tumor.

procedures.name: Enucleation
procedures.approach: Complete removal of the eye, usually with insertion of an orbital implant.
indications: Large tumors not suitable for plaque brachytherapy (largest diameter >19 mm, thickness >10 mm, or thickness >8 mm with optic nerve involvement).
Intraocular recurrence not amenable to other therapies.
key_complications: Postoperative pain, hemorrhage, nausea/vomiting, cardiovascular or pulmonary problems, urinary retention, fever.
procedures.operative_mortality: Not specified in the source text.

procedures.name: Orbital Exenteration
procedures.approach: Surgical removal of the globe and adjacent orbital contents.
indications: Visible extraocular tumor at time of enucleation.
procedures.operative_mortality: Not specified in the source text.

surgery.resectability_criteria: Not explicitly defined. The decision is based on tumor size, location, extension, and whether globe-conserving therapy is appropriate.
surgery.pre_operative_optimisation: Not specified in the source text.

radiation_therapy.role: Radiation therapy is the primary treatment modality for most patients with uveal melanoma, with the goal of achieving local tumor control while preserving the eye. Plaque brachytherapy and particle beam therapy are standard definitive options for primary tumors. Stereotactic radiosurgery (SRS) is used less frequently as primary treatment. Fractionated photon radiotherapy is used as adjuvant therapy after surgery for orbital involvement. Metastasis-directed radiotherapy is used for palliation or treatment of limited metastases.
principles: Radiation therapy should be performed by an experienced multidisciplinary team including an ophthalmic oncologist, radiation oncologist, and physicist (brachytherapy or particle beam).

Tumor localization for brachytherapy may be performed using indirect ophthalmoscopy, transillumination, light pipe diathermy, and/or ultrasound (intraoperative and/or preoperative). MRI or CT may be used for preoperative planning.
For particle beam therapy, fiducial markers (tantalum clips) are encouraged to permit eye and tumor position verification.
Volumetric planning in 3 dimensions is encouraged to maximize radiation delivery to tumor and minimize radiation to organs at risk.

dose_frameworks.name: Plaque Brachytherapy (Iodine-125 COMS plaques)
dose_frameworks.schedule: Over approximately 5-7 days (duration of plaque wear)
dose_frameworks.indication: Primary treatment for tumors with largest basal diameter ≤19 mm and thickness ≤10 mm.

dose_frameworks.name: Plaque Brachytherapy (Other radioisotopes or non-COMS I-125 plaques)
dose_frameworks.schedule: Varies by isotope and plaque type
dose_frameworks.indication: Primary treatment for tumors with largest basal diameter ≤19 mm and thickness ≤10 mm.

dose_frameworks.name: Particle Beam Therapy (Protons)
dose_frameworks.indication: Primary treatment for intraocular tumors, especially those not suitable for plaque brachytherapy.

dose_frameworks.name: Photon Stereotactic Radiosurgery (SRS)
dose_frameworks.indication: Primary treatment for large primary tumors or intraocular recurrence. Less commonly used as primary treatment.

dose_frameworks.name: Photon Stereotactic Radiosurgery (SRS)
dose_frameworks.indication: Primary treatment for large primary tumors or intraocular recurrence. Less commonly used as primary treatment.

dose_frameworks.name: Adjuvant Fractionated Photon Radiotherapy (IMRT)
dose_frameworks.indication: Adjuvant to surgery for orbital involvement (margin-positive enucleation or exenteration) or resected regional metastases.

approaches.name: Plaque Brachytherapy
approaches.dose_fractionation: 85 Gy to tumor apex at low dose-rate (≥0.6 Gy/h) using iodine-125 COMS plaques.
approaches.indication: Primary treatment for tumors with largest basal diameter ≤19 mm and thickness ≤10 mm.
approaches.key_trial: Collaborative Ocular Melanoma Study (COMS) randomized trial.
toxicities: Radiation retinopathy, optic neuropathy, papillopathy, maculopathy.

approaches.name: Particle Beam Therapy
approaches.dose_fractionation: 50–70 CGyE in 4–5 fractions (protons) or 60–85 CGyE in 5 fractions (carbon ions).
approaches.indication: Primary treatment for intraocular tumors, especially those not suitable for plaque brachytherapy.
approaches.key_trial: Randomized trials comparing plaque brachytherapy and particle beam therapy.
toxicities: Decrease in visual acuity, loss of vision.

approaches.name: Photon Stereotactic Radiosurgery (SRS)
approaches.dose_fractionation: 45–70 Gy in 2–5 fractions (fractionated) or 18–45 Gy in 1 fraction (single-fraction).
approaches.indication: Primary treatment for large primary tumors or intraocular recurrence. Less commonly used as primary treatment.
toxicities: Higher rates of neovascular glaucoma, radiation retinopathy, and radiation papillopathy compared to brachytherapy.

approaches.name: Fractionated Photon Radiotherapy (Adjuvant)
approaches.dose_fractionation: 20–30 Gy in 5 fractions using IMRT with image guidance.
approaches.indication: Adjuvant to surgery for orbital involvement (margin-positive enucleation or exenteration) or resected regional metastases.
approaches.toxicities: Not specified separately for this indication.

systemic_therapy.overview: Systemic therapy for metastatic uveal melanoma is challenging due to limited efficacy compared to cutaneous melanoma. The only FDA-approved agent with a demonstrated overall survival benefit is tebentafusp-tebn, which is limited to HLA-A*02:01-positive patients. Other options include immune checkpoint inhibitors (with lower response rates than in cutaneous melanoma), cytotoxic chemotherapies, and targeted therapies (e.g., trametinib). Treatment selection depends on HLA-A*02:01 status, performance status, disease pattern (hepatic-dominant vs. non-hepatic-dominant), and availability of clinical trials.

by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Clinical trial (preferred). For HLA-A*02:01-positive disease: Tebentafusp-tebn (Category 1). For HLA-A*02:01-negative disease or if tebentafusp is not preferred/appropriate: Immune checkpoint inhibitors (e.g., ipilimumab + nivolumab).
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Tebentafusp-tebn demonstrated an overall survival benefit in a phase III trial in previously untreated HLA-A*02:01-positive metastatic uveal melanoma. Immune checkpoint inhibitors have activity in a minority of patients.
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: If disease is hepatic-dominant, liver-directed therapies (e.g., percutaneous hepatic perfusion, embolization) should be considered. Since tebentafusp-tebn response rates are low, symptomatic patients may be better palliated by liver-directed treatment first or their disease may respond better to ipilimumab/nivolumab. Cytotoxic regimens (e.g., dacarbazine, temozolomide, paclitaxel) or targeted therapy (e.g., trametinib) are useful in certain circumstances.
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: HLA-A*02:01 testing is required for tebentafusp-tebn eligibility.

by_stage_and_setting.setting: Metastatic or Unresectable Disease, Subsequent Lines
by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Clinical trial (preferred). Options include immune checkpoint inhibitors (ipilimumab, anti-PD-1 monotherapy like pembrolizumab or nivolumab), or other regimens.
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Agents effective for metastatic cutaneous melanoma may be used after disease progression with first-line tebentafusp-tebn for HLA-A*02:01-positive disease.
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Cytotoxic regimens (dacarbazine, temozolomide, paclitaxel, albumin-bound paclitaxel, carboplatin/paclitaxel) or targeted therapy (trametinib).

by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Liver-directed therapies (e.g., regional isolation perfusion with melphalan percutaneous hepatic perfusion, embolization, ablative procedures, resection and/or RT) in combination with or followed by systemic therapy.
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: If disease is confined to the liver, regional therapies are recommended.
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Systemic therapies or best supportive/palliative care.

key_regimens.name: Tebentafusp-tebn
key_regimens.setting: First-line for metastatic uveal melanoma in HLA-A*02:01-positive patients.
key_toxicities: Cytokine release syndrome (especially first few cycles, requiring hospitalization).
Most treatment-related adverse events occur early.
Treatment discontinuation due to adverse events was low (2%).

key_regimens.name: Ipilimumab + Nivolumab
key_regimens.setting: First-line or subsequent-line for metastatic uveal melanoma.
key_regimens.cycle_days: Every 3 weeks for 4 cycles (induction), followed by nivolumab maintenance.
key_toxicities: Grade 3 or 4 treatment-related adverse events occurred in 40% of patients in one study.
Profiles consistent with known toxicities of checkpoint inhibitors.

key_regimens.name: Pembrolizumab (monotherapy)
key_regimens.setting: First-line or subsequent-line for metastatic uveal melanoma.

key_regimens.name: Nivolumab (monotherapy)
key_regimens.setting: Subsequent-line for metastatic uveal melanoma after progression on ipilimumab.

key_regimens.name: Ipilimumab (monotherapy)
key_regimens.setting: First-line or subsequent-line for metastatic uveal melanoma.
key_regimens.cycle_days: Induction: every 3 weeks for 4 cycles. Maintenance: every 12 weeks.
key_toxicities: Grade 3–4 adverse events reported in 10-36% of patients.

key_regimens.name: Dacarbazine
key_regimens.setting: Useful in certain circumstances for metastatic uveal melanoma.

key_regimens.name: Temozolomide
key_regimens.setting: Useful in certain circumstances for metastatic uveal melanoma.

key_regimens.name: Paclitaxel
key_regimens.setting: Useful in certain circumstances for metastatic uveal melanoma.

key_regimens.name: Albumin-bound Paclitaxel
key_regimens.setting: Useful in certain circumstances for metastatic uveal melanoma.

key_regimens.name: Carboplatin/Paclitaxel
key_regimens.setting: Useful in certain circumstances for metastatic uveal melanoma.

key_regimens.name: Trametinib
key_regimens.setting: Useful in certain circumstances for metastatic uveal melanoma.
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Standard MEK inhibitor toxicities (e.g., rash, diarrhea, cardiomyopathy).

treatment_response_assessment.title: Assessment of Response to Treatment for Metastatic Disease
treatment_response_assessment.timing: Following treatment for metastatic disease, patients should receive imaging to assess response or progression.
response_logic: Imaging modalities for assessment are the same as those recommended for initial staging (e.g., MRI abdomen with IV contrast preferred, CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with IV contrast, whole-body FDG-PET/CT).
After treatment, patients with no evidence of disease (NED) may be eligible for adjuvant clinical trials.
If post-treatment imaging shows residual or progressive disease, the NCCN Panel recommends trying other options for treatment of distant metastatic disease.
imaging_recommendations: MRI abdomen with IV gadolinium-based contrast (preferred).
CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with IV iodinated contrast.
CT chest with or without IV contrast.
In select patients with renal failure and/or iodinated contrast allergy, MR abdomen with gadolinium-based contrast is preferred over CT.
Abdominal imaging should be performed with IV contrast unless contraindicated.
biopsy_or_salvage_logic: Biopsy to confirm extraocular recurrence or metastasis should be performed histologically whenever possible or if clinically indicated.
Biopsy techniques may include core needle biopsy or fine-needle aspiration (FNA).
Consider genetic testing if it might affect treatment options (e.g., eligibility for tebentafusp).
For intraocular recurrence, biopsy can often be diagnosed without a biopsy, but additional prognostic FNA biopsy may be valuable.
